fields.name: Password Hashing
fields.intro_template:
## Password Hashing

Storing passwords: salt them, slow the hash way down. PBKDF2, bcrypt, and Argon2id are the three industry standards. Argon2id is the modern default.

fields.prompt_template:
## Password hashing — the threat model

You run a website. Users sign up with email + password. Where do those passwords live?

**Not in plaintext.** Database backups get stolen. Insider threats happen. Misconfigured S3 buckets happen. If your database leaks and the passwords are plaintext, every user's account on every other site (where they reused the password) is gone too.

**Not as raw SHA-256, either** — and that's the part that needs unpacking. SHA-256 looks like the obvious move: one-way, deterministic, fixed-size output. The problem is what "fast" buys an attacker once your DB has leaked.

### Why hashes-for-integrity are wrong for passwords

| Property | Good for file checksums | Bad for password storage |
|---|---|---|
| Fast | Yes — checksum a 4GB ISO in seconds | Yes — try 10⁹ passwords per second on a GPU |
| Deterministic | Yes — same file, same hash | Yes — and that's a problem too (see rainbow tables) |
| No salt | Doesn't matter for a file | Catastrophic: every user with `password123` has the same hash |

A single RTX 4090 computes ~10 billion SHA-256 hashes per second. A leaked database of 1 million SHA-256-hashed passwords using common words falls in **minutes**. Modern dedicated cracking rigs (8-GPU racks) push that to ~10¹¹/s.

### Three defenses, three primitives

Password hashing exists to make the attacker's job impossibly expensive. Three orthogonal defenses make that work:

1. **Per-user salt.** A random 16-byte value stored alongside the hash. The hash becomes `H(password || salt)`. Two users with the same password get different hashes. **Rainbow tables die** — an attacker would need to precompute a separate table per user, defeating the whole shortcut.
2. **Deliberate slowness.** Iterate the hash thousands or millions of times. A single login takes 100ms (invisible to the user); a cracking rig now tries ~10 guesses per second per core instead of 10⁹. **The cost ratio flips by 8 orders of magnitude.**
3. **Memory-hardness.** Require the hash to allocate megabytes of RAM per evaluation. GPUs and ASICs are fast on compute but cramped on memory bandwidth. **Forces the attacker to use the same hardware as you** — commodity CPUs with lots of RAM.

Three industry-standard algorithms package these defenses:

- **PBKDF2** (1999, RFC 8018) — salt + iteration count. NIST-approved, FIPS-compliant. No memory-hardness.
- **bcrypt** (1999) — built on the Blowfish key schedule, exponential cost factor. Battle-tested. No memory-hardness, 72-byte password limit.
- **Argon2** (2015, PHC winner) — memory-hard, tunable across time/memory/parallelism. The modern default.

This lesson walks each one, runs PBKDF2 live in your browser, and ends with a side-by-side timing chart and a recommendation matrix you can ship with.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** Your users' passwords live in a database that will eventually leak. Plaintext is obviously bad. Raw SHA-256 is almost as bad — fast hashes are exactly what GPU cracking rigs are built for. The fix is three combined ideas: a random per-user salt, deliberate slowness, and memory-hardness. The lesson teaches each one.

fields.prompt_template:
### The naive failure — SHA-256 of `password123`

The widget on this page hashes the literal string `"password123"` using `crypto.subtle.digest("SHA-256", ...)` — the same Web Crypto call from the [SHA-256 lesson](/algorithms/sha256/learn/walk-the-hash/). It captures the wall-clock time via `performance.now()`.

Result: **64 hex characters in ~0.1 ms**, give or take. That's the SHA-256 of one of the most-leaked passwords in history. Anyone with a copy of the [rockyou.txt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RockYou) wordlist has this digest precomputed.

### The threat in numbers

| Setup | Cost per guess | Time to crack 6-char lowercase |
|---|---|---|
| Web Crypto in your browser | ~0.1 ms | 89 days |
| Server CPU with SHA-NI | ~50 ns | 26 minutes |
| Single RTX 4090 (~10⁹ guesses/s) | ~0.1 ns | **3 seconds** |
| 8-GPU rack (~10¹¹ guesses/s) | ~0.01 ns | **0.03 seconds** |

26⁶ = 308,915,776 candidate passwords. At 10¹¹ guesses/second, that search space is gone in a third of a second. Add digits and the 7-char space (62⁷ ≈ 3.5×10¹²) still falls in under a minute.

### What the rest of this lesson fixes

The issue is not SHA-256. SHA-256 is *correctly* fast — it's what you want for file integrity, TLS certificate fingerprints, Bitcoin proof-of-work, and HMAC. It is correctly the wrong primitive for storing passwords.

The rest of the lesson swaps SHA-256 for primitives whose *whole design point* is being slow:

- **PBKDF2** (step 3) — iterate HMAC-SHA-256 a configurable N times. Live in your browser.
- **bcrypt** (step 4) — exponential cost factor on the Blowfish key schedule. Cited timings.
- **Argon2id** (step 5) — memory-hard, GPU-resistant. Cited timings.
- **Side-by-side** (step 6) — five timings on a log scale, so you can *see* the gap.

> The fact that a hash is fast is not a flaw of the hash. It's a flaw of using a hash for the wrong job.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** SHA-256 hashing `"password123"` is fast. Fast is the *whole problem* for password storage. An attacker with a GPU farm guesses ~10 billion SHA-256s per second; if their target hash is in the rainbow tables they don't even need to guess. The fix is to slow the hash down (PBKDF2, bcrypt, Argon2id) and salt it.

fields.prompt_template:
### PBKDF2 — slow it down with iteration count

**PBKDF2** (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2, RFC 8018, 1999) is the simplest of the three slow-hash families. The construction is one parameter and one knob:

```
PBKDF2(password, salt, iter, hash) =
    HMAC(password, salt || 0x00000001) iterated `iter` times, XOR-chained
```

The inner primitive is **HMAC-SHA-256** (covered in the [HMAC lesson](/algorithms/hmac/learn/mac-the-message/)). Each iteration runs HMAC-SHA-256 over the previous round's output. Doubling `iter` doubles the wall-clock time — for the legitimate verifier *and* the attacker.

### Where PBKDF2 shines

- **FIPS compliance.** PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 is the only password hash on the NIST short list (SP 800-132). bcrypt and Argon2 are not.
- **Stdlib everywhere.** Python (`hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac`), Java (`PBKDF2WithHmacSHA256`), .NET (`Rfc2898DeriveBytes`), Web Crypto. No third-party install.
- **Tunable in one number.** Bump `iter` whenever your CPU gets faster.

### Where PBKDF2 falls short

- **Not memory-hard.** GPUs and ASICs can parallelize PBKDF2 — each guess takes the same amount of memory as a single SHA-256, which is essentially nothing. Argon2 closes this gap.
- **Linear cost.** Doubling the work means doubling the iteration count. bcrypt's cost factor is exponential — `cost +1` doubles work, so it scales more gracefully as hardware improves.

### Recommended iteration counts

| Year | Source | Recommendation (PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256) |
|---|---|---|
| 2010 | NIST SP 800-132 | ≥1,000 |
| 2017 | NIST SP 800-63B | ≥10,000 |
| 2023 | OWASP | ≥600,000 |
| 2024 | 1Password | 650,000 |

The trend line is clear: as commodity hardware speeds up, iteration count goes up too. PBKDF2 is the only one of the three slow-hashes whose recommended parameter has *increased by ~3 orders of magnitude over 15 years*.

### The widget on this page

Type any password (printable ASCII, up to 200 chars). Pick an iteration count from the dropdown — `1,000` / `10,000` / `100,000` / `1,000,000`. On submit, your browser calls:

```js
const salt = crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(16));
const baseKey = await crypto.subtle.importKey(
  "raw", new TextEncoder().encode(password),
  {name: "PBKDF2"}, false, ["deriveBits"]
);
const bits = await crypto.subtle.deriveBits(
  {name: "PBKDF2", hash: "SHA-256", salt, iterations: N},
  baseKey, 256
);
```

The result is a 32-byte (256-bit) derived key. The widget shows the hex, the salt, and the wall-clock time. **Submit again at a higher iteration count** — the time should scale roughly linearly. That's the cost-vs-security knob.

**The salt is not a secret.** It's stored alongside the hash in your DB. Its only job is to make every user's hash unique even when the passwords are identical, killing rainbow tables.
fields.help_template:
**In plain English:** PBKDF2 = HMAC-SHA-256 iterated N times. Type a password, pick a number of iterations, watch the time scale linearly. The salt is the random 16 bytes — it's stored next to the hash and is not secret, just unique-per-user. The output (64 hex chars = 32 bytes) is what your database actually stores.

**Practical numbers:** NIST 2017 said 10,000+. OWASP 2023 says 600,000+. Bump the number whenever your CPUs get faster.

fields.prompt_template:
### bcrypt — Blowfish's key schedule, on purpose

**bcrypt** (Provos and Mazières, 1999, originally for OpenBSD) takes a wildly different approach from PBKDF2. Instead of iterating a hash function, bcrypt iterates the **key schedule of Blowfish** — a phase of the cipher that's already deliberately slow because it has to set up the 4 KB of S-boxes and the P-array Blowfish uses for encryption.

If you've done the [Blowfish lesson](/algorithms/blowfish/learn/), this is the same key schedule — repurposed. bcrypt iterates it `2^cost` times. The whole hash output is structured like this:

```
$2b$12$Rj4Xb1n6q0KQpD8eYxLuKO6yL.YH8q3eRdkLW3v8w5xZcGZH5wG3K
  ^  ^  ^                       ^
  |  |  |                       └─ 31-char base64-encoded hash
  |  |  └─ 22-char base64-encoded salt (128 bits)
  |  └─ cost factor (2^12 = 4096 key-schedule iterations)
  └─ algorithm version tag
```

One string, embedded everything. Storage is trivial — no separate salt column.

### The exponential cost knob

bcrypt's cost factor is **logarithmic**: cost `c` means `2^c` iterations of the key schedule. Each `+1` to the cost factor doubles the work.

| Cost | Iterations | Cited time on a 2024 laptop |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 1,024 | ~65 ms |
| 11 | 2,048 | ~125 ms |
| 12 | 4,096 | ~250 ms |
| 13 | 8,192 | ~500 ms |
| 14 | 16,384 | ~1 s |
| 15 | 32,768 | ~2 s |

**The 2024 default is cost 12.** Most Ruby on Rails apps, PHP frameworks (Symfony, Laravel), and Node.js stacks ship with cost 10-12 as the default. Bump to 13 or 14 if you can afford the login latency.

### Why no live bcrypt in this browser?

A faithful JavaScript bcrypt implementation (e.g., `bcryptjs`) is **~30 KB of code** and pegs the main thread for hundreds of milliseconds per hash at cost 12. We don't ship it in this lesson — the cited timing column above is what you'd measure with `bcrypt` on Python (the C-backed `bcrypt` package), with `bcryptjs` in Node, or with `BCryptPasswordEncoder` in Spring Boot. The PBKDF2 demo in step 3 already lets you *feel* the linear-cost relationship; bcrypt's exponential one only matters when comparing widely separated cost factors.

### Limitations to know

- **72-byte password truncation.** bcrypt silently truncates anything longer than 72 bytes. Some libraries warn, some don't. If you accept passphrases, pre-hash with SHA-256 before bcrypt to dodge the limit.
- **No memory-hardness.** bcrypt uses ~4 KB of state per evaluation — small enough that a GPU can run thousands in parallel. Argon2id closes this gap.
- **Null-byte handling.** Some old bcrypt implementations treat a null byte as end-of-string, which can cause hash collisions on weird inputs. The 2b version tag fixes this; older `$2a$` hashes may be affected.

### When to use bcrypt anyway

- You already have bcrypt deployed. **Stay on bcrypt at cost 12+** unless you have a concrete reason to migrate; the migration is non-trivial because old hashes must be re-derived at next login.
- You need a battle-tested algorithm. bcrypt has 25 years of cryptanalysis behind it and no known practical attacks.
- You're in a stack where Argon2 isn't natively supported and pulling in the dependency is more risk than reward.

New deployments? Argon2id, covered in step 5.
fields.help_template:
**In plain English:** bcrypt is Blowfish's setup routine, run thousands of times. Cost 12 = 4096 iterations ≈ 250 ms. Output is a single string with the cost, salt, and hash baked in. It's not memory-hard, has a 72-byte password limit, and we don't run it live here because a real JS bcrypt is 30 KB and blocks the page for half a second per hash.

**Practical guidance:** Use bcrypt cost 12+ if you already have bcrypt; Argon2id is the better choice for new systems.

fields.prompt_template:
### Argon2 — memory-hard, GPU-resistant

**Argon2** (Biryukov, Dinu, Khovratovich, 2015) won the **Password Hashing Competition** — a 4-year, 9-finalist bake-off explicitly run to find a successor to bcrypt and PBKDF2. It's the modern default for new systems.

Argon2's headline feature is **memory-hardness**: the algorithm forces each evaluation to allocate megabytes of RAM and reference them in a pattern that defeats time-memory tradeoffs. A GPU has thousands of cores but limited per-core memory bandwidth — exactly the kind of hardware Argon2 is designed to handicap.

### Three tunable parameters

| Knob | Meaning | RFC 9106 recommended default |
|---|---|---|
| `m` (memory) | Megabytes of RAM per hash | 64 MB (or 19 MB for low-memory devices) |
| `t` (time) | Number of passes over the memory block | 3 |
| `p` (parallelism) | Threads / lanes | 4 |

A 64 MB × 3-pass evaluation costs about 500 ms on a typical 2024 laptop and forces an attacker to allocate 64 MB *per concurrent guess*. A GPU with 24 GB of VRAM can fit ~370 concurrent Argon2id guesses; the same GPU running PBKDF2 fits hundreds of thousands.

### Three variants

Argon2 publishes three flavors, each tuned for a different threat:

| Variant | Memory access pattern | Strength | Weakness | Use when |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Argon2d** | Data-dependent | Best GPU resistance | Vulnerable to side-channel attacks (cache timing) | Server-side, trusted hardware, no co-tenants |
| **Argon2i** | Data-independent | Side-channel-resistant | Weaker against tradeoff attacks | Untrusted hardware, multi-tenant cloud |
| **Argon2id** | Hybrid: `i` for first pass, `d` for rest | Best of both | Slightly more complex | **Default for almost everything** |

RFC 9106 (2021) recommends **Argon2id** for general-purpose password hashing. Unless you have a specific reason to pick `d` or `i`, use `id`.

### The compression primitive

Argon2 uses **Blake2b** as its internal compression function. Blake2 is a hash designed for speed (faster than SHA-2, comparable to SHA-3) and is what makes Argon2's per-block cost cheap enough that the bottleneck is memory access, not compute. You don't need to know Blake2b to use Argon2 — it's a black box at this layer — but the lineage is worth flagging: every Argon2 hash is ultimately a Blake2b chain over a pseudorandom-walked block of RAM.

### Where Argon2 shows up

- **1Password, Bitwarden, Standard Notes** — encrypted at rest with Argon2id-derived keys.
- **Signal** — uses Argon2id for PIN-derived keys.
- **WireGuard** — pre-shared key derivation.
- **Phabricator, Bitwarden, FreeBSD's pw(8)** — password storage.
- **OWASP top recommendation** for new password storage as of 2023.

### Why no live Argon2 in this browser?

A WebAssembly Argon2 build (e.g., `argon2-browser`) is ~150 KB and *needs* its memory-hardness to actually be hard — running it at default parameters allocates 64 MB and blocks the main thread for ~500 ms per call. We don't ship it. The cited timing in step 6 is what you'd measure with `argon2-cffi` on Python (`PasswordHasher().hash(p)`) on a typical 2024 laptop.

The codegen on the Done step uses `argon2-cffi` for the verify-roundtrip demo.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** Argon2id is the modern default. It's memory-hard, meaning each guess takes ~64 MB of RAM — which slows down GPU attackers way more than it slows down the legitimate server. Three knobs: memory, time, parallelism. RFC 9106 recommends m=64MB, t=3, p=4. Pick Argon2id unless you have a specific reason to pick d or i.

fields.prompt_template:
### Side-by-side — five timings, log scale

The widget on this page hashes the demo password `"correct horse battery staple"` five different ways and renders a bar chart of the wall-clock cost per evaluation. Three of the rows are computed **live in your browser**; two are **cited** from published benchmarks (we don't ship live bcrypt or Argon2 — see steps 4 and 5).

| Row | Algorithm | Cost basis |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SHA-256** | Live via `crypto.subtle.digest` |
| 2 | **PBKDF2-1k** | Live via `crypto.subtle.deriveBits` |
| 3 | **PBKDF2-100k** | Live via `crypto.subtle.deriveBits` |
| 4 | **bcrypt cost 12** | Cited (~250 ms on a typical 2024 laptop) |
| 5 | **Argon2id default** | Cited (~500 ms on a typical 2024 laptop) |

### Read the chart on a log scale

SHA-256 finishes in **microseconds**. Argon2id-default takes **half a second**. On a linear axis, SHA-256 would render as a single pixel next to a screen-wide Argon2id bar. The widget plots `log10(ms)` so you can see all five rows in the same frame.

Expected shape:

```
SHA-256             ▏                                           <1 ms
PBKDF2-1k           ▌▌                                          ~1 ms
PBKDF2-100k         █████                                       ~100 ms
bcrypt cost 12      ██████ (cited)                              ~250 ms
Argon2id default    ████████ (cited)                            ~500 ms
```

### What the gap means

A password verification cost of ~500 ms is **invisible to the user at login** (the network roundtrip is already comparable). But for an attacker with a leaked database:

- A consumer GPU running raw SHA-256 cracks at ~10⁹ guesses/second.
- The same GPU running Argon2id-default cracks at ~10² guesses/second (memory-hardness ceiling).
- **That's a 10⁷ slowdown of the attacker** for the same fraction-of-a-second cost at login.

That single ratio is the entire reason this lesson exists. A user logs in once a session. An attacker guesses billions of times. The asymmetry pays for itself.

### About the cited rows

- **bcrypt cost 12: ~250 ms.** Source: `bcrypt` (Python) benchmarks on Intel/AMD laptop CPUs, 2024.
- **Argon2id default: ~500 ms.** Source: `argon2-cffi` defaults (m=64MB, t=3, p=4) on the same class of hardware. Cited as a placeholder hash string in the format `$argon2id$v=19$m=65536,t=3,p=4$<placeholder>` — what `PasswordHasher().hash()` would actually emit.

The `(cited)` badges on the bar chart make this explicit. The live rows compute real hashes against the demo password every time you load the page.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** SHA-256 takes microseconds. PBKDF2-100k takes ~100 ms. bcrypt-12 takes ~250 ms (cited). Argon2id-default takes ~500 ms (cited). The chart is on a log scale because SHA-256 would be invisible next to Argon2id otherwise. That 10⁷-× gap between SHA-256 and Argon2id is why password hashing exists as a separate primitive.

fields.prompt_template:
### Which one to use — recommendation matrix

You now know the three primitives. The hard part is shipping. Below is the recommendation matrix used by OWASP, NIST SP 800-63B, RFC 9106, and most security-conscious shops in 2024.

| Situation | Pick | Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **New system, no constraints** | **Argon2id** | `m=64MB, t=3, p=4` (RFC 9106 defaults) |
| **Existing bcrypt deployment** | Stay on bcrypt | Cost `≥12`; bump to 13-14 if latency budget allows |
| **FIPS / NIST-only environment** | PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 | `iter ≥ 600,000` (OWASP 2023) |
| **Embedded / memory-constrained device** | Argon2id with reduced `m` | `m=19MB, t=2, p=1` (RFC 9106 low-memory profile) |
| **Multi-tenant cloud, side-channel risk** | Argon2i (or Argon2id) | Same defaults as Argon2id |
| **Legacy migration (MD5, SHA-1, raw SHA-256)** | **Argon2id over re-hashed legacy** | Re-derive on next login: store `Argon2id(legacy_hash, salt)`, mark for upgrade |

### Things you should never do

- **Plaintext.** This shouldn't need saying. It also shouldn't have appeared at LinkedIn (2012), Adobe (2013), and yet here we are.
- **Raw SHA-256, SHA-1, MD5.** Fast hashes are the wrong primitive. We spent the whole lesson on this.
- **Single-iteration HMAC.** HMAC is a MAC, not a password hash. The `key` slot is wrong for a salt.
- **Custom homebrew (`md5(md5(password) + salt)` etc.).** Cryptographers spent four years comparing nine submissions in the PHC. They picked Argon2id. Trust them.
- **`scrypt`** — not wrong, just superseded. scrypt (Percival, 2009) was the first widely-deployed memory-hard hash. Argon2id is its conceptual descendant and won the PHC bake-off. If you already use scrypt at sane parameters, you're fine; if you're picking now, pick Argon2id.

### Constant-time comparison — a sidebar that bites people

The verification step compares two hashes:

```python
# WRONG
if stored_hash == computed_hash: ...
```

Most language `==` operators **short-circuit on the first byte that differs**. A timing-savvy attacker can measure how long the comparison took and learn how many leading bytes of their guess match the stored hash. That's enough to brute-force the hash one byte at a time, in linear-not-exponential work.

```python
# RIGHT — Python
import hmac
if hmac.compare_digest(stored_hash, computed_hash): ...
```

```javascript
// RIGHT — Node
import { timingSafeEqual } from "node:crypto";
if (timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(stored), Buffer.from(computed))) ...
```

Both functions run in constant time regardless of where the first difference is. `bcrypt.checkpw` and `PasswordHasher.verify` both do this internally; if you're rolling your own verify on a raw PBKDF2 output, use the constant-time comparator. The codegen on the Done step shows both.

### Operational reminders

- **Bump your cost parameters over time.** Hardware speeds up. The OWASP recommendation for PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 grew 60× between 2017 and 2023.
- **Re-hash on login if the cost parameter has changed.** Detect old hashes (parameters are in the stored string for bcrypt and Argon2), verify, then re-derive at the new cost and update storage.
- **Add a pepper if your threat model includes DB-only leaks.** A site-wide secret XOR'd or concatenated with the password before hashing, stored *outside* the database (env var, KMS, HSM). Out of scope for this lesson — but worth knowing exists.
- **Rate-limit login attempts.** Password hashing slows offline attackers; rate limiting slows online ones. Both, not either.
fields.help_template:
**In plain English:**

- **New system?** Argon2id with the RFC 9106 defaults (`m=64MB, t=3, p=4`).
- **Have bcrypt already?** Keep bcrypt, cost 12+.
- **FIPS-only?** PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256, iter ≥ 600,000.
- **Never:** plaintext, MD5, SHA-1, raw SHA-256, homebrew.

And: use `hmac.compare_digest` (Python) or `crypto.subtle.timingSafeEqual` (Node) for any hash comparison. `==` leaks timing.

fields.prompt_template:
## Done!

You've walked the password-hashing problem from the threat model to a shippable recommendation. Recap:

### The three defenses, seen in this lesson

| Defense | First met in | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-user salt** | Step 1 (threat model) and step 3 (PBKDF2 widget) | Defeats rainbow tables. Two users with the same password get different hashes. Salt is not secret. |
| **Deliberate slowness** | Step 3 (PBKDF2 iter count) and step 4 (bcrypt cost) and step 5 (Argon2 time cost) | Caps the attacker's guess rate. ~100 ms at login is invisible; ~10⁹ guesses/s on a GPU is ruinous. |
| **Memory-hardness** | Step 5 (Argon2 memory cost) | Defeats GPU/ASIC acceleration. Forces the attacker to spend the same RAM you do. |

### The five algorithms, ranked

| | Live in this lesson? | Memory-hard? | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw SHA-256** | Yes (step 2) | No | Never for passwords |
| **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256** | Yes (steps 3 + 6) | No | FIPS-compliant fallback only, iter ≥ 600k |
| **bcrypt** | No (cited) | No | Fine for existing deployments at cost ≥ 12 |
| **Argon2id** | No (cited) | **Yes** | **Default for new systems** (RFC 9106) |
| **scrypt** | Not covered | Yes | Superseded by Argon2id |

### Forward link — HKDF

PBKDF2 stretches a **password** (low-entropy human input) into a key. **HKDF** (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function, RFC 5869) does the same starting from an **already-strong key** — a shared secret from Diffie-Hellman, a master key from an HSM, etc. Different threat model, different construction. PBKDF2 spends thousands of iterations to slow brute-force; HKDF skips that work entirely because the input was never weak. (HKDF is a future lesson.)

### Run it yourself

Click **Show full script** below. The codegen emits a Python file using `argon2-cffi` and `bcrypt`. It hashes the demo password `"correct-horse-battery-staple"` under both algorithms, verifies the roundtrip succeeds, and verifies that the wrong password is rejected (raises for `argon2`, returns `False` for `bcrypt`).

```bash
pip install argon2-cffi bcrypt
python password_hashing_demo.py
```

For a single-library shop that needs to handle legacy migrations across MD5/SHA/bcrypt/Argon2 in one codebase, look at [`passlib`](https://passlib.readthedocs.io/) — it's the multi-algorithm wrapper Django and many web frameworks use under the hood.
fields.help_template: **In plain English:** Salt + slow + memory-hard. Argon2id by default, bcrypt if you already have it, PBKDF2 if you must be FIPS-compliant. Never raw SHA-256, never plaintext, never your own scheme. Use the codegen below to see what the Python implementation looks like end-to-end.
